title: Prader-Willi Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
content: Most affected individuals exhibit behavioral and psychiatric manifestations, including temper outbursts, obsessive–compulsive features, skin picking, rigidity, and, in some cases, mood or psychotic disorders, with about a quarter of children meeting the diagnostic criteria for autism spectrum disorder. [21] Adolescents and adults with mood disorders or psychoses may benefit from medication and therapy. Behavioral strategies help patients understand rules, schedules, expectations, and verbal cues, thereby minimizing symptoms of compulsion and aggression. [9] Skin picking is a frequent behavior, and a study has demonstrated the effectiveness of treatment with oral guanfacine. [22] Cognitive impairment affects many individuals, and adults may experience accelerated cognitive decline with age. Schoolchildren should have an individualized educational plan, while adults often benefit from a supervised vocational setting.

title: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Location of the Treatment
content: Babies with NAS have long received treatment in the NICU, and this has been a traditional practice rather than evidence-based. According to the PHIS database, about 87% of neonates with NAS were managed in the NICU. [14] While the NICU admission may be necessary because of the severity of symptoms or comorbidities, the environment is not ideal because of multiple distractions and could be overstimulating to these infants. Rooming-in, which promotes mother-infant bonding, skin-to-skin, and breastfeeding, is currently the preferred model of care as it has correlated with reduced severity of NAS. [54] [56] This activity can be best achieved in the postnatal ward, and 1 study showed a consequent reduction in the duration of pharmacotherapy and LOS. [43] Another quality improvement study proved the same results in a general inpatient pediatric ward. [1] Thus, it may be time to reconsider the age-old practice of admitting all infants to a critical care setting to manage NAS. Some studies have also evaluated the possibilities of outpatient only and combined inpatient-outpatient management and found a relative reduction in LOS and costs with outpatient management. However, outpatient management was associated with increased days on pharmacotherapy, and concerns exist among physicians in handing over opioid prescriptions to caregivers of those infants who are at risk of relapse. [16]

title: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome -- History and Physical
content: CNS symptoms typically include hyperirritability, high-pitched crying, jitteriness, and tremors. Hyperirritability leads to sleep disturbances and difficulty maintaining a calm state. [39] Myoclonic jerks and seizures are also possible. The occurrence of seizures secondary to withdrawal has been debatable; in the past, the frequency of seizures in NAS has been between 2% and 11%. Interictal EEGs have found it to be myoclonic jerks rather than true seizures, and many recent studies corroborate the rare occurrence of seizures secondary to withdrawal. [40] [41] Autonomic instability leads to changes in vital signs such as tachycardia, tachypnea, and hyper- or hypothermia. Skin perfusion becomes altered, leading to mottling and sweating. Frequent yawning, nasal stuffiness, excessive sneezing, and nasal flaring may also be present. Some of these may persist for months, especially in cases of withdrawal from buprenorphine. [38]

title: Lesch-Nyhan Syndrome -- History and Physical
content: Self-injurious behavior is the hallmark of Lesch Nyhan syndrome. Self-mutilation is not seen in the variants of Lesch Nyhan syndrome. The most commonly noted behavior is self-mutilation from biting of lips, fingers, cheeks, causing profound disfigurement. This behavior usually begins with the eruption of teeth in infants. Contrary to the thought that patients continue with self-mutilation due to sensory deficits, all sensations, including pain, are intact. [5] Self-injurious behavior is a result of compulsive behavior, and restraints can control these actions. Behaviors like head-banging, limb-banging, eye-poking, which are usual presentations in other disorders, have also been described. Reports have also mentioned compulsive aggressive behavior where patients try to inflict injury to others by pinching, spitting, grabbing, hitting, or verbally abusing. [6] [7] Once the compulsion passes away, they usually tend to apologize for their behavior.

title: Methylphenidate -- Monitoring
content: Children receiving methylphenidate should have their growth curves closely monitored for a stable progression in height and weight, as this medication is known to suppress growth when used daily and long-term. The medication should either be readjusted or discontinued if children are not in a healthy percentile on their growth curve. [22] Clinicians should screen for symptoms of depression, agitation, aggressiveness, new-onset or pre-existing psychosis or mania, and suicidality, as these can be exacerbated during treatment initiation. Clinicians should also monitor for signs of intravenous abuse as frank psychotic episodes can develop. Clinicians should look for peripheral vasculitis (digital ulceration) during physical exams.

title: Echolalia -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Gilles de la Tourette syndrome: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a childhood-onset neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by vocal and motor tics. Echolalia, coprolalia, and echopraxia are some features of TS. Motor tics present in TS include jerks, twitches, and tics. The disorder has a wide range of severity and is associated with other behavioral and psychiatric disorders. TS is often associated with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and obsessive-compulsive disorder. Tourette syndrome has a waxing and waning course. Cognitive-behavioral therapy is the first-line treatment for TS. Pharmacotherapy is used if behavioral therapy alone does not work. Medications like clonidine, risperidone, fluoxetine have been used. [28]

title: Hypomelanosis of Ito -- Treatment / Management
content: Management of HOI is primarily symptomatic, focusing on the specific clinical manifestations present in each patient. The cutaneous findings typically do not require medical treatment; however, cosmetic camouflage may be helpful for individuals with aesthetic concerns. Sometimes, the hypopigmented areas may fade over time, with partial or complete resolution observed in adolescence or adulthood. Early intervention is critical for addressing developmental or cognitive delays, and anticonvulsant therapy is typically necessary for seizure management. Ongoing follow-up is essential to monitor for emerging complications. Clinicians should offer genetic counseling and referrals to appropriate specialists to ensure comprehensive care. [25]

Based on tolerability and response, 1 patch may be added at weekly or longer intervals, up to a maximum of 30 mg per day. Some patients may need up to 60 mg per day for optimal efficacy. The patch can be removed before 9 hours if a shorter duration of action is desired or if late-day side effects occur. Plasma concentrations typically decline once the patch is removed, but drug absorption may continue for several hours afterward.

title: Body Dysmorphic Disorder -- Introduction
content: The DSM has recategorized BDD since its introduction in 1980. Initially described as an atypical somatoform disorder and then a distinct somatoform disorder in 1987, BDD now falls in the DSM-5-TR under the spectrum of obsessive-compulsive and related disorders. [1] Patients affected by BDD have a preoccupation with a perceived defect or flaw in their physical appearance that is either not noticeable or only slightly observable by others. Symptoms cause marked impairment in social, academic, occupational, or other areas of functioning. To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must engage in repetitive behaviors, such as excessive mirror checking, camouflaging (ie, covering up the defect with makeup or clothing), skin picking, excessive grooming, excessive weight lifting, or pervasive mental acts such as comparing one’s appearance to others. These behaviors are time-consuming, difficult to control, and distressing to the individual. [2] Body dysmorphic disorder is underrecognized, and affected patients visit dermatology and plastic surgery clinics to repair their perceived defects. Understanding BDD's features and diagnostic criteria across all healthcare specialties helps increase awareness and recognition of the condition. [3]

title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1297
content: Amphetamine, atomoxetine, methylfenidát (methylphenidate) and modafinil are all classified as centrally acting sympathomimetics. As members of this pharmacologic class, each drug exerts its therapeutic effects primarily by enhancing central neurotransmitter activity, particularly catecholamines such as norepinephrine and dopamine. This shared mechanism underlies their common use in disorders that benefit from increased central arousal and attention, including attention‑deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and narcolepsy. Although they belong to the same class, the individual agents differ in their specific pharmacodynamic profiles and clinical indications, with amphetamine and methylfenidát being traditional stimulants, atomoxetine acting as a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, and modafinil promoting wakefulness through a distinct, though still sympathomimetic, pathway. Recognizing these drugs as centrally acting sympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their overlapping effects, potential side‑effect patterns, and appropriate therapeutic contexts.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1550
content: Centrally acting sympathomimetics is a pharmacologic class defined by stimulation of central nervous system pathways that increase sympathetic tone. The class includes the selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor atomoxetine, the potent stimulant methamphetamine, the dopamine‑ and norepinephrine‑reuptake inhibitor methylphenidate, and the wake‑promoting agent modafinil. Although these agents share a common mechanism of central sympathomimetic activity, they differ in chemical structure, potency, and clinical indications. Atomoxetine is primarily prescribed for attention‑deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and carries a lower abuse potential than classic stimulants. Methamphetamine, a highly potent sympathomimetic, is associated with significant abuse risk and is used medically only in limited contexts. Methylphenidate, also used for ADHD and narcolepsy, exerts its effect by blocking dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake. Modafinil promotes wakefulness in narcolepsy and shift‑work sleep disorder and has a comparatively lower risk of cardiovascular stimulation. Recognizing that atomoxetine, methamphetamine, methylphenidate, and modafinil belong to the centrally acting sympathomimetics class helps clinicians anticipate shared side effects such as increased heart rate, blood pressure, and potential anxiety, while allowing therapy to be tailored to each drug’s specific pharmacologic profile and abuse potential.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 528
content: Centrally acting sympathomimetics are a subclass of psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics, and they are included within the broader category of psychoanaleptics. This class comprises specific drugs such as atomoxetine, methylfenidát, and modafinil, each of which is a member of the centrally acting sympathomimetics group. Because psychostimulants are themselves classified as psychoanaleptics, centrally acting sympathomimetics inherit the properties of both psychostimulant activity and psychoanaleptic effects, meaning they stimulate central nervous system function and are employed to improve attention, focus, and cognitive performance. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians understand how individual agents like atomoxetine, methylfenidát, and modafinil fit into larger therapeutic categories and guides appropriate selection of medication for ADHD management and cognitive enhancement.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 479
content: Centrally acting sympathomimetics are a subgroup of psychostimulants that are employed as agents for attention‑deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and as nootropics. This class encompasses several well‑known compounds, including atomoxetine, methamphetamine, methylphenidate, and modafinil, each of which is considered a member of the centrally acting sympathomimetic group. Psycholeptics (often referred to as “psycho‑analeptics” in older terminology) are pharmacologically distinct from both anti‑dementia drugs and psychostimulants; they do not belong to the anti‑dementia drug category nor to the psychostimulant/ADHD‑nootropic category. Within the anti‑dementia drug domain, the “Other anti‑dementia drugs in ATC” category is itself a subclass of anti‑dementia drugs, and memantine is identified as a member of this specific subgroup. Together, these relationships clarify the hierarchical organization of these medication classes, highlighting that centrally acting sympathomimetics function as ADHD‑focused psychostimulants, psycholeptics occupy a separate therapeutic class, and memantine belongs to a defined subset of anti‑dementia agents.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a type of anti‑dementia drug and function as cholinesterase inhibitors that block acetylcholinesterase activity. Donepezil is a specific member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug group. In addition to anticholinesterase agents, other anti‑dementia drugs in the ATC system include memantin, which is also a member of the broader anti‑dementia drug class. Both anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs and other anti‑dementia drugs fall under the larger category of psychoanaleptics, a class that is inversely related to psychostimulants and agents used for ADHD and nootropic purposes. Psychostimulants, in turn, are represented by the subclass of centrally acting sympathomimetics, which are defined as psychostimulants used for ADHD and nootropic effects. Centrally acting sympathomimetics include atomoxetine, methamfetamin (methamphetamine), methylfenidát (methylphenidate), and modafinil as individual members. Thus, the pharmacological landscape links anti‑dementia therapies—particularly cholinesterase‑inhibiting agents like donepezil and NMDA‑receptor antagonists like memantin—to the broader psychoanaleptic group, which shares classification overlap with central sympathomimetic psychostimulants employed in the treatment of attention‑deficit disorders and cognitive enhancement. This interconnected taxonomy aids clinicians in understanding the shared mechanisms and therapeutic categories across cognitive, stimulant, and neuropsychiatric drug classes.
